rationale_for_baseline_tier: A patient with a known mast-cell disorder has a severe but skin-confined acute urticarial flare: widespread pruritic wheals, dermatographism and flushing, with a deliberate multi-system red-flag screen that is NEGATIVE (no throat tightness/voice change/dysphagia, no dyspnoea/wheeze, no faintness, no GI symptoms) and normal haemodynamics. ASCIA anaphylaxis criteria are not met, so this is NOT a T4/T5 emergency. The standard OTC antihistamine has failed, so it also is not pure self-care — it needs prompt clinical management (H1 up-titration + H2 antagonist, cooling, trigger avoidance) that a telehealth encounter can initiate now, with GP review within ~3 days. That is a T2 (prompt outpatient) disposition. Critically, the tier is paired with MANDATORY anaphylaxis safety-netting: an explosive cutaneous mast-cell flare can, uncommonly, be the leading edge of an evolving systemic reaction, so escalation to T5/000 is pre-armed on any airway or circulatory feature. Over-escalating the stable baseline flare to ED/000 is over-triage and the primary scored defect.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Baseline severe cutaneous flare with a negative systemic red-flag screen (throat, breathing, circulation, GI all clear)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Initiate antihistamine escalation and cooling now; GP review within about 3 days
escalation_edges.rationale: Skin-confined mast-cell flare in a known mast-cell patient: prompt outpatient management with antihistamine escalation is appropriate; ED/000 is not indicated for the stable baseline.
escalation_edges.communication_template: This looks like a bad skin flare of your mast-cell condition, not a whole-body allergic emergency. Let's step up your antihistamines and cool you down now, and get you a GP review within about 3 days — but I want you to know exactly when to call 000, which I'll go through now.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Any throat tightness, difficulty swallowing, voice change, or new wheeze / shortness of breath
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call 000 immediately — within minutes, do not wait
escalation_edges.rationale: Any airway or lower-respiratory involvement converts a cutaneous flare into evolving anaphylaxis — an immediate life threat requiring emergency ambulance and adrenaline.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your throat starts to feel tight, your voice changes, you struggle to swallow, or you start wheezing or can't catch your breath — call 000 straight away and tell them you may be having an allergic reaction affecting your breathing. If you have an adrenaline autoinjector, use it into your outer thigh.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Feeling faint, dizzy, lightheaded on standing, or a sense of impending collapse
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call 000 immediately and lie down with legs raised
escalation_edges.rationale: Faintness signals possible distributive (anaphylactic) shock; immediate 000, supine positioning and adrenaline if available are required.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you feel faint, dizzy or like you might pass out, lie down and raise your legs and call 000 immediately — that can be a sign the reaction is affecting your blood pressure.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Flare keeps spreading and is not settling despite the stepped-up antihistamines and cooling over the next few hours, or repeated vomiting develops
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek urgent same-day in-person review (urgent care or GP) the same day
escalation_edges.rationale: A cutaneous flare that fails to respond to escalated therapy, or that gains a GI limb, warrants urgent same-day in-person assessment even before it meets emergency criteria.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the welts keep spreading and aren't settling after a few hours despite the stepped-up antihistamines and cooling, or you start vomiting, get seen in person the same day at an urgent care or your GP.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone and is highly anxious about the airway; no one else is present to observe deterioration
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Because she is alone, the safety-net must be extra explicit and concrete: keep the phone within reach, keep the front door unlocked, and if in ANY doubt about breathing or faintness call 000 rather than waiting. Offer a shorter telehealth re-contact or lower the threshold for same-day in-person review given there is no second observer.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Known mast-cell activation disorder with prior flares
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Her established pattern supports recognising this as a recurrent cutaneous flare, but a known mast-cell disorder also carries a real (if uncommon) risk of a future systemic reaction — reinforce a written anaphylaxis action plan and discuss whether she should carry an adrenaline autoinjector with her specialist.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is a severe skin flare of your mast-cell condition, not a whole-body allergic emergency right now. Step up your non-drowsy antihistamine and add the stomach-acid-type antihistamine as advised, take a cool (not cold) shower, wear loose cotton, and cool the room down. See your GP within about 3 days to fine-tune the medications. BUT call 000 immediately if your throat tightens, your voice changes, you can't swallow, you start wheezing or get short of breath, or you feel faint.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Telehealth re-contact or GP within 3 days for the flare; call 000 immediately for any airway or faintness symptoms.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 is available 24 hours a day for airway or circulatory symptoms. For a flare that is worsening but with no airway/faintness features, use after-hours GP or an urgent care clinic the same evening.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Document the heat-triggered cutaneous mast-cell flare, the negative multi-system anaphylaxis screen, the OTC antihistamine failure, the stepped-up H1/H2 plan, and the anaphylaxis safety-net given. Recommend GP consider a mast-cell stabiliser, an ASCIA anaphylaxis action plan, and specialist immunology review.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the flare as trivial 'just hives', giving pure self-care and closing the encounter with NO anaphylaxis safety-net and no escalation of the failed antihistamine.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: If the flare evolves to airway or circulatory involvement, a patient living alone with no safety-net does not know to call 000 and could suffer airway compromise unrecognised.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Routing to routine GP in 1–2 weeks without stepping up therapy tonight or emphasising the immediate anaphylaxis safety-net for a severe, OTC-refractory acute flare.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Labelling the skin-confined flare as anaphylaxis and escalating to adrenaline/ED/000 at baseline despite a fully negative airway/respiratory/circulatory/GI screen — the primary scored defect of this over-triage-resistance case.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether an explosive cutaneous mast-cell flare will remain skin-confined or evolve into a systemic reaction/anaphylaxis, in a patient who cannot be physically examined for airway/chest findings via telehealth.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Exclude anaphylaxis on the patient-reportable multi-system screen, manage the cutaneous flare as outpatient (T2), and PRE-ARM the patient with explicit, time-bound 000 escalation instructions for any airway or circulatory feature — safety by safety-netting, not by over-escalation.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Resolving the uncertainty by defaulting to a full anaphylaxis response (adrenaline + 000) for a stable cutaneous flare, OR by falsely reassuring and closing without any escalation instructions.
